title: Educate yourself with The Bee Guy's daily basic bee facts
author: Jennifer Sandlin
summary: I recently stumbled across "The Bee Guy," who provides fun and educational content on his social media, including daily basic bee facts. I'm learning so much about bees through his posts! Did you know, for instance, that bumblebees travel great distances by hitchhiking on passenger airlines? —
full_text:
I recently stumbled across "The Bee Guy," who provides fun and educational content on his social media, including daily basic bee facts. I'm learning so much about bees through his posts! Did you know, for instance, that bumblebees travel great distances by hitchhiking on passenger airlines? Researchers in France found that queens, males, and worker female bees all engage in this behavior. Wild stuff! Or that springtime queen bees are larger but lighter than their autumn counterparts? This is because queens lose 80% of their body fat during the winter, and larger bumblebees are more likely to survive hibernation. I also learned that humans, bees, primates, and some new world monkeys all have trichromatic vision, meaning that we all see three colors of light. However, while we (and our primate and monkey relatives) perceive the colors blue, green, and red, bees see green, blue, and ultraviolet.

The Bee Guy, who provides these basic bee facts, along with super cool videos and photos of all kinds of bees, is named Paul, and he and his partner Clare-Louise run the World Bee Sanctuary in Coolafancy, County Wicklow, Ireland. They call themselves the "Bonnie and Clyde of native wild bee conservation" and describe the sanctuary as the "one and only native wild bee sanctuary on the planet." They engage in education and advocacy in order to save endangered native wild bees.

According to Ohio State University entomologist and bee expert Dr. Reed Johnson, pollinators like bees, butterflies, bats, and more contribute about $500 billion a year to global food production. Of all the pollinators, bees stand out as some of the most important, as they pollinate about a third of the world's food supply. They're super important, obviously, but across the globe some bee populations are declining rapidly, due to urbanization and the use of herbicides, pesticides, and insecticides. Dr. Johnson suggests a few ways to help preserve healthy bee populations: create bee-friendly outdoor spaces by planting a diverse selection of bee-attractive blooming flowers and trees in your yard; eat foods grown without pesticides; purchase local honey; avoid using insecticides, herbicides, and pesticides on your lawn and in your garden; and, when you see bees, leave them alone — please don't kill our pollinator friends!

Learn more about the World Bee Sanctuary at their website, and follow them on Instagram for more daily basic bee facts.
